RT by @lookner: A reminder of what the US waiver revocation on Iranian oil sales means -- and doesn't mean: Iran can still sell its oil if it finds a willing buyers (and China has been in the past, and will be again). Yes, Iran will need to discount its oil, but that would be mostly offset by the rise in oil prices triggered by the fresh hostilities. The biggest problem for Iran? It would have to use (again) non-US dollar settlement. Put it all together, and Tehran would be able to sell the ~60 million barrels it has put on the water so far since the blockade was lifted, at relatively good prices (~$65-70 a barrel), making probably ~$4 billion. Only a return to the US Navy blockade would stop Iran from selling its oil. But that means a new war.

RT by @no_itsmyturn: If the Trump administration is serious about restoring pressure on Iran, it should do more than just strike at military targets. The U.S. should sanction the banks that enable Iran — not just the front companies they serve. During Economic Fury, Treasury warned regulators in China, Hong Kong, Oman, and the UAE, and even sent warning letters to two Chinese banks. But those warnings were never followed by sanctions. The first target should be China’s Bank of Kunlun. Treasury confirmed as recently as August 2025 that Bank of Kunlun remains a key piece to moving funds from Beijing to Tehran. In 2012, the U.S. cut Bank of Kunlun off from correspondent account access, but that was largely symbolic. The bank had little exposure to the U.S. financial system. This time should be different. Treasury should designate Bank of Kunlun under EO 13902 and make clear to its parent, China National Petroleum Corporation (CNPC is the one of the world’s largest oil companies), that continuing to operate the bank carries real risk. That’s what Maximum Pressure should look like.
